evidence: Public interface methods are domain-aligned and consistently formed: executeCollocations, executeCqlQuery, executeBcqlQuery, executeDependencyPattern, getTotalFrequency — all verb+noun phrases that communicate what the operation does and what it returns

Internal record types are well-chosen: CollocateSearch, HitRecord, AggregatedCollocateStats, SeedCollocateData, CollocateAccumulator — each name ties the type to the specific stage or purpose in the pipeline

Port/interface naming follows a coherent taxonomy: CollocateQueryPort, SketchQueryPort, QueryExecutor, ExplorationService — narrow ports have narrow names, the full executor is named to reflect its combined role
Exploration-layer classes are descriptively named: SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, SingleSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator — names map directly to algorithm roles
Handler method names are consistent and action-oriented: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, handleRelationsForType, routeSketchRequest, handleRelationQueryForPattern

CONFIRMED OPEN: executeCollocations (verb+noun-plural) vs executeSurfacePattern (verb+noun-phrase) on QueryExecutor — inconsistent naming grammar for two parallel collocate-lookup operations on the same interface

CONFIRMED OPEN: GroupStats (private record in BlackLabQueryExecutor) — name does not reveal what is grouped or what statistics it holds; in context it holds collocate frequency map and lemma-to-POS map extracted from HitGroups

NEW: collocateFrequency in SingleSeedExplorer.identifyCoreCollocates (line 181) is a Map<String,Integer> counting how many DiscoveredNouns share each collocate — not a corpus frequency at all. In a corpus-linguistics codebase 'frequency' is a loaded term referring to corpus occurrence count; using it for a noun-sharing count is a vocabulary mismatch. The parallel variable in MultiSeedExplorer uses the clearer name collocateSharedCount.

naming_quality.dimension_character: The codebase demonstrates strong naming discipline overall: domain vocabulary is used consistently across packages, method names are action-oriented and specific, and internal data-carrying types are named for their role in the pipeline rather than their structure. The three outstanding issues share a common root — naming precision slips at internal/private scope where the cost of a bad name feels lower, but the gap between the two public interface methods is the most visible problem.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Strong majority of names clearly communicate intent with correct domain vocabulary; the codebase would score higher if not for the asymmetric interface-method grammar (module-visible), the opaque GroupStats private record, and the misleading collocateFrequency local variable in a domain where 'frequency' has a precise technical meaning. Score reflects good-but-not-great: most names are right, a few have real ambiguity, none are egregiously generic or misleading at the public API level.

issues.summary: executeCollocations and executeSurfacePattern use inconsistent naming grammar on QueryExecutor
evidence: executeCollocations: verb + noun-plural — describes the thing returned
executeSurfacePattern: verb + noun-phrase — describes the mechanism, not the thing returned

Both methods do collocate-lookup over an index; their naming should follow the same grammar, e.g. executeCollocations / executeSurfacePatternCollocations, or executeCollocates / executeSurfacePatternCollocates
issues.suggestion: Rename executeSurfacePattern to executeSurfacePatternCollocations (or alternatively rename both to a verb+mechanism grammar: executeFromPattern / executeFromCql). Pick one grammar and apply it to all collocate-lookup methods on the interface.

issues.summary: GroupStats private record in BlackLabQueryExecutor does not communicate what is grouped or what statistics it holds
evidence: private record GroupStats(Map<String, Long> freqMap, Map<String, String> lemmaPosMap) — name says 'group statistics' but fields reveal it is collocate co-occurrence frequencies plus a lemma-to-POS map
Used in collectFrequenciesAndPosFromGroups which returns a GroupStats — the method name is more descriptive than the type name
A name like CollocateExtractionResult or CollocateFrequencyData would make the return type self-documenting
issues.suggestion: Rename GroupStats to CollocateExtractionResult or CollocateFrequencyData to reflect what the record holds (co-occurrence frequencies and POS annotations extracted from HitGroups).

issues.summary: collocateFrequency in SingleSeedExplorer.identifyCoreCollocates counts noun-sharing, not corpus frequency
evidence: Line 181: Map<String, Integer> collocateFrequency = new LinkedHashMap<>()
Line 185: collocateFrequency.merge(collocate.getKey(), 1, Integer::sum) — increments by 1 per DiscoveredNoun that shares this collocate
This counts how many nouns in the semantic field co-occur with each collocate — not corpus frequency
In contrast, MultiSeedExplorer uses collocateSharedCount for the same concept (in SeedCollocateData), which is precise

In this codebase 'frequency' and 'freq' consistently refer to corpus occurrence counts (wordSketchResult.frequency(), getTotalFrequency, headwordFreq, collocateFreq); this variable breaks that convention
issues.suggestion: Rename collocateFrequency to collocateNounCount or collocateSharedNounCount in identifyCoreCollocates to match the naming used in MultiSeedExplorer and to distinguish it from corpus frequency.

root_causes: Naming precision degrades at private/local scope: public API names are consistently domain-accurate, but private records and local variables allow ambiguous terms (GroupStats, collocateFrequency) to slip through

The interface naming asymmetry (executeCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern) likely reflects evolutionary growth — the second method was added with a different framing without auditing the existing method name grammar

add.header: Strong public API naming; slippage at private scope
add.description: Public interface and exported-class method names are domain-accurate and consistently formed. Naming quality degrades at private-record and local-variable scope, where three known issues live. This pattern suggests a naming culture that prioritises the public contract but does not apply the same scrutiny to internal data carriers.

add.header: frequency/freq vocabulary is corpus-frequency in this codebase
add.description: Throughout the codebase 'frequency', 'freq', and 'Freq' consistently refer to corpus occurrence count (getTotalFrequency, headwordFreq, collocateFreq, WordSketchResult.frequency). Any local variable named *frequency that holds a count of something else (e.g. noun-sharing count) breaks this convention and should be renamed.
